does: This paper introduces the first systematic benchmark for evaluating whether LLMs can identify security risks by analyzing system logs from Model Context Protocol servers. The authors define nine categories of MCP server risks and construct a dataset of 2,421 training chat histories and 471 evaluation queries by embedding 1,800 synthetically-generated system logs into the return values of 243 curated MCP servers. The logs are created using ten state-of-the-art LLMs to ensure diversity and realism. Through pilot experiments, the paper demonstrates that smaller LLMs frequently fail to detect risky system behaviors, establishing baseline performance metrics and identifying detection gaps across model scales and risk categories.
connects: This work extends existing research on LLM security in tool-use and integrated systems. Prior benchmarks have concentrated on prompt injection attacks and vulnerabilities within LLM-MCP interaction trajectories, but largely overlooked the signal available in system-level logging from potentially compromised servers. The paper builds on the broader literature examining LLM agent safety when interfacing with external tools and APIs, particularly as the Model Context Protocol has emerged as a standard for such interactions. It complements research on LLM interpretability and capability assessment by focusing specifically on a security-critical but understudied dimension: whether models can perform risk inference from low-level system artifacts rather than high-level instructions or demonstrations.
enables: This benchmark enables future work on improving LLM robustness and interpretability in safety-critical tool-use scenarios. Researchers can develop and evaluate methods to enhance LLM anomaly detection in system logs, potentially through fine-tuning, retrieval-augmented generation, or multi-modal analysis combining logs with other signals. The work opens investigation into what log patterns models can and cannot recognize, informing both log design standards and LLM architectural improvements for security applications. It also sets a foundation for building automated security monitoring systems where LLMs assist in real-time log analysis, and encourages development of better pre-training and fine-tuning strategies that improve risk inference capabilities across model scales.
